Gmail Hidden Tips You Probably Don't Know

Gmail's everywhere — pretty much everyone has one, and we constantly use Gmail to sign up for services (GitHub, AWS, Heroku, you name it). This post covers two underused Gmail tricks: the plus-alias trick for managing signups, and Undo Send for catching mistakes after you hit Send.

Since most of us use Gmail daily, we've all hit the moment of "wait, I forgot the attachment" or "oh no I sent that to the wrong person". Gmail's Undo Send feature gives you a brief window to take it back.
Under "General", find "Undo Send" and set it to 30 seconds (30 seconds to second-guess yourself should be enough, right?):
After that, every time you hit Send, you'll see a prompt with an "Undo" button. You've got 30 seconds before it ships for real.
Honestly saves you from missing-attachment embarrassment more often than you'd think.

Most of us use Gmail to sign up for services — GitHub, AWS, Heroku, and so on. This post covers two underused Gmail features that make signup and sending safer: the plus-suffix alias trick (yourname+something@gmail.com) for managing per-service signups and spotting leaks, and Gmail's Undo Send delay so you can claw a badly-sent email back.
